critic_raw:
This design compares a TCN against linear regression for ED wait time prediction, with particular focus on weekend and holiday performance.

The dataset is restricted to 'encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months.' This selection filters out first-time and low-frequency ED visitors. ED wait time in practice is driven largely by capacity constraints (staffing, bed availability, patient volume) that affect all patients equally, but the selected cohort is systematically older, sicker, and more complex than the general ED population. More critically, the exclusion of first-time visitors removes a large fraction of weekend and holiday encounters, where many patients are using the ED for the first time due to the unavailability of primary care. The hypothesis specifically claims improvement on 'weekend and holiday data,' but the dataset selection most aggressively removes the weekend/holiday-specific population. The design therefore tests the hypothesis on exactly the wrong patient cohort.

Second, the rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed 'from the full dataset statistics.' For a temporal split, this means the rolling arrival statistic applied to training-period encounters uses arrival patterns from the validation and test periods to calibrate the rolling mean. This is particularly harmful for a temporal split because arrival volumes likely differ across months 1-16 (training), 17-20 (validation), and 21-24 (test) — using full-dataset statistics for a temporally ordered feature directly leaks future arrival patterns into training features.

Third, the TCN uses input sequences of length 12 representing the '12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in.' These sequences are constructed from the arrival history — which is a property of all patients, not just the selected cohort. The design uses a dataset filtered to patients with prior visits but constructs arrival-based features from this filtered population's arrival patterns. The filtered arrival patterns do not represent the total ED load, which is what determines wait times. If total arrival volume is not available (because the dataset excludes first-time visitors), the arrival-based features are systematically underestimating queue load.

Fourth, the statistical significance test for the primary comparison is 'a paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors.' A paired t-test assumes that per-encounter prediction errors are normally distributed and independent. For ED encounter data, encounters are temporally autocorrelated (busy hours cluster), and absolute errors are non-negative with likely right-skewed distributions. The paired t-test is not robust to these distributional assumptions, and a Wilcoxon signed-rank test would be more appropriate.
all_issues_raised: The dataset is restricted to patients with prior ED visits in the last 12 months, excluding first-time and low-frequency visitors; weekend and holiday encounters disproportionately include first-time visitors seeking care due to primary care unavailability, so the dataset selection removes exactly the subpopulation central to the hypothesis's focus on weekend/holiday improvement.

The rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from full-dataset statistics despite the temporal split; arrival volume patterns from validation and test periods contaminate the training-period arrival feature, leaking future load information.

The TCN's arrival-sequence features are constructed from the filtered cohort's arrival patterns rather than total ED arrivals; filtered-cohort arrival volume underestimates true queue load because the majority of walk-in and first-time patients (who drive wait times) are excluded from the dataset.

The paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors assumes normality and independence; ED encounter errors are temporally autocorrelated and right-skewed, making the t-test anti-conservative and its p-values unreliable.
all_issues_adjudicated: The dataset is restricted to patients with prior ED visits in the last 12 months, excluding first-time and low-frequency visitors; weekend and holiday encounters disproportionately include first-time visitors seeking care due to primary care unavailability, so the dataset selection removes exactly the subpopulation central to the hypothesis's focus on weekend/holiday improvement.

The rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from full-dataset statistics despite the temporal split; arrival volume patterns from validation and test periods contaminate the training-period arrival feature, leaking future load information.

The TCN's arrival-sequence features are constructed from the filtered cohort's arrival patterns rather than total ED arrivals; filtered-cohort arrival volume underestimates true queue load because the majority of walk-in and first-time patients (who drive wait times) are excluded from the dataset.

The paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors assumes normality and independence; ED encounter errors are temporally autocorrelated and right-skewed, making the t-test anti-conservative and its p-values unreliable.
